response:
Section: Governance Automation (Wisdm)

Purpose
- Define the required governance automation capabilities to be delivered as part of the Wisdm program. The goal is automated, auditable enforcement of cloud governance controls mapped to NIST CSF, automated remediation of drift, centralized visibility via workbooks, and integration with Wisdm remediation tasks and platform components (Azure Policy, Azure Monitor Workbooks, Data Collection Rules).

Scope
- Governance automation must cover the tenant and management group hierarchy, all subscriptions in scope, resource groups and resources, and applicable platform services. It must enforce and continuously monitor policy compliance, trigger automated remediation when safe and applicable, and provide management reporting and auditing.

Standards & Alignment
- All automation and policy artifacts must be explicitly mapped to NIST CSF categories and sub-controls.
- Use Azure-native policy primitives (Azure Policy definitions, initiatives, built-in effects: Deny, Modify, DeployIfNotExists, Append) as primary enforcement mechanisms.
- Ensure OS categorization (Windows / Linux / Other) is automated and feeds Data Collection Rules (DCR) assignment for monitoring and telemetry collection.

Functional Requirements
- Policy Authoring & Packaging
  - Provide policy definitions and initiatives (policy-as-code) stored in a source control repository (GitHub/Azure Repos).
  - Policy artifacts must include human-readable rationale, NIST CSF mapping, remediation approach (effect type), and testing artifacts.
- Automated Assignment & Scope
  - Automate assignment of initiatives to management groups and subscriptions using GitOps/CICD (pull requests -> automated deployment).
  - Support scoping and exclusions per subscription/resource group/resource with clear inheritance.
- Automated Remediation
  - Where possible, implement auto-remediation using DeployIfNotExists/Modify or managed remediation tasks (Azure Policy remediation, Logic Apps, Azure Functions, Automation Runbooks).
  - Integrate Wisdm remediation tasks so remediation executes from the platform and reports status back to the control plane.
- Drift Detection & Scheduling
  - Continuous evaluation at configurable intervals; support on-demand policy evaluation and scheduled full scans.
- Exceptions & Approvals
  - Provide formal exemption workflow integrated with ticketing system (ServiceNow/Jira) and record exemptions in policy metadata with expiration.
- OS Categorization & DCR Integration
  - Automate application of OS category tags/attributes and create/assign DCRs for telemetry collection according to OS classification rules.
- Inventory & Baseline
  - Maintain a centralized inventory of policies, assignments, remediation tasks, and compliance state.

Technical & Implementation Requirements
- Policy-as-Code & CI/CD
  - Store policy code in version-controlled repository. Implement CI checks, unit tests (policy validation), and automated promotion to environments.
- Deployment Mechanism
  - Use GitOps or automation pipelines to deploy policy initiatives and remediation runbooks to target scopes.
- Identity & Access
  - Use managed identities for remediation tasks. Enforce least privilege via Azure RBAC. Support delegated management scenarios (Azure Lighthouse).
- Logging & Telemetry
  - All policy evaluations, remediation actions, and exceptions must be logged to a central Log Analytics workspace and forwarded to SIEM/Event Hub.
- Monitoring & Dashboards
  - Provide pre-built Azure Monitor Workbooks that map policy compliance and remediation state to NIST CSF controls and operational KPIs (compliance %, time-to-remediate, number of exceptions).
- Scalability & Performance
  - Solution must scale across hundreds of subscriptions and report evaluation latencies and remediation throughput.

Monitoring, Reporting & KPIs
- Minimum dashboards and reports:
  - Compliance summary per management group/subscription mapped to NIST CSF.
  - Remediation status and success/failure rates.
  - Drift frequency and time-to-remediate metrics.
  - Exception inventory with age and approver history.
- Alerts
  - Automated alerts for policy fail-high events, remediation failures, and repeated non-compliance. Integrate with existing alerting channels and ticketing systems.

Security & Controls
- Policy artifacts and CI/CD pipelines must be access-controlled and auditable.
- Remediation runbooks/functions must operate under managed identity with narrowly scoped permissions.
- Sensitive telemetry must be protected in transit and at rest; Log Analytics retention settings and access policies required.

Testing, Validation & Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver test playbooks and evidence showing:
  - Automated assignment of initiatives to management groups and subscriptions.
  - Successful auto-remediation for at least three representative DeployIfNotExists/Modify scenarios.
  - OS categorization applied and DCRs created/assigned for Windows and Linux hosts in test scope.
  - Workbooks displaying compliance mapped to NIST CSF for an agreed sample.
  - End-to-end ticketing/exemption workflow demonstration.
- Acceptance requires: demonstrable compliance improvement in pilot scope, successful execution of automated remediation tasks, complete logging, and functioning workbooks.

Deliverables
- Policy definitions and initiatives (source repo).
- CI/CD pipeline for policy deployments.
- Automation runbooks/Functions/Logic Apps for remediation.
- Azure Monitor Workbooks and reporting templates.
- Implementation and runbook documentation, test results, and runbook handover.
- Training session for operations and governance teams.

Evaluation Criteria
- Completeness of NIST CSF mapping.
- Use of Azure-native services (Policy, Workbooks, Log Analytics) and alignment with Wisdm remediation framework.
- Quality of automation, testing, and operational handover.
- Extensibility and maintainability of policy-as-code and CI/CD pipeline.

Service Levels & Support
- Define remediation execution SLAs (e.g., auto-remediate within X hours for medium risk).
- Provide operational support and maintenance plan for policies, workbooks, and remediation tasks.

Note: If additional organizational standards (e.g., tagging taxonomy, change control process, or specific ticketing integrations) exist, they must be incorporated into the policy definitions and automation workflows during implementation.
